%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 1.4-1 The Unit Step Function u(t)

> **Section Objective:** Learn how the unit step function u(t) turns signals on, off, shifts them in time, and represents piecewise waveforms.

## Concepts In This Section

- Unit step function
- Shifted steps
- Rectangular pulse
- Piecewise signal gating
- Reflected step u(-t)

## 1. The unit step: an on-switch at t=0

Think of \(u(t)\) as a light switch. Before \(t=0\), the switch is **off** — the signal value is 0. At \(t=0\) and for all time after, the switch is **on** — the signal value is 1. The textbook uses the convention \(u(0)=1\).

**Minimal example:**
- \(u(-2) = 0\) because \(-2 < 0\)
- \(u(3) = 1\) because \(3 \ge 0\)

#### Exam Trigger
Whenever a signal starts at a specific time, expect a unit step to appear in the expression.

#### Common Misuse
Do not treat \(u(t)\) like a ramp. It does **not** grow gradually — it jumps from 0 to 1 **instantly** at \(t=0\).

$$u(t)=\begin{cases}0, & t<0\\1, & t\ge 0\end{cases}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. Build a rectangular pulse by subtracting steps

Two shifted steps can create a pulse that is on for a finite interval and zero everywhere else.

- \(u(t-2)\) **turns the signal on** at \(t=2\): it is 0 before \(t=2\) and 1 after.
- \(-u(t-4)\) **turns the signal off** at \(t=4\): it subtracts 1 starting at \(t=4\).

**Interval-by-interval check:**

| Interval | \(u(t-2)\) | \(-u(t-4)\) | \(x(t)\) |
|----------|-----------|------------|----------|
| \(t < 2\) | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| \(2 \le t < 4\) | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| \(t \ge 4\) | 1 | \(-1\) | 0 |

After \(t=4\), the two terms cancel exactly, returning the signal to zero.

#### Exam Trigger
A flat pulse from \(a\) to \(b\) almost always appears as \(u(t-a) - u(t-b)\).

#### Common Misuse
Writing \(u(t+2) - u(t+4)\) shifts the pulse to the **left** (to \(-2\) through \(-4\)), not to \(t=2\) through \(t=4\).

$$x(t)=u(t-2)-u(t-4)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="eyJjcmFtIjoiUmVjb2duaXplIHRoZSBwdWxzZSBhcyAndHVybiBvbiBhdCAyLCB0dXJuIG9mZiBhdCA0Jy4iLCJzdGFuZGFyZCI6IkNvbm5lY3QgdGhlIGZvcm11bGEgeCh0KT11KHQtMiktdSh0LTQpIHRvIHRoZSBncmFwaCBwYW5lbCBieSBwYW5lbC4iLCJ0b3Bfc2NvcmUiOiJVc2UgdGhlIGNvbXBvbmVudCBwbG90IHRvIGV4cGxhaW4gY2FuY2VsbGF0aW9uIGFmdGVyIHQ9NC4ifQ==" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. 1.15](/figures/page-083-fig__1_15-1.png)
*Fig. 1.15 shows how \(x(t) = u(t-2) - u(t-4)\) creates a unit-height rectangular pulse from \(t=2\) to \(t=4\).*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The figure contains two time-domain plots. Panel (a) shows the resulting signal x(t): it is zero before t=2, rises to height 1 at t=2, stays at 1 until t=4, then drops back to zero — a clean rectangular pulse. Panel (b) shows the two component step functions separately: u(t-2) jumps up to 1 at t=2 and stays there, while -u(t-4) jumps down to -1 at t=4. Students should notice that after t=4, the two components sum to 1+(-1)=0, confirming that the second step cancels the first and the pulse ends cleanly at t=4 without producing a negative value in the final signal.</div>

## 3. Gate each piece of a piecewise signal

A bracket of the form \([u(t-a) - u(t-b)]\) acts as a **time window**: it equals 1 only between \(t=a\) and \(t=b\), and 0 everywhere else. Multiplying any formula by this bracket keeps that formula active only on the interval \([a, b]\) and shuts it off outside.

**Reading the triangle example:**

- **First piece:** \(t \cdot [u(t) - u(t-2)]\) — the ramp \(t\) is active from \(t=0\) to \(t=2\) only.
- **Second piece:** \(-2(t-3) \cdot [u(t-2) - u(t-3)]\) — the line \(-2(t-3)\) is active from \(t=2\) to \(t=3\) only.

**Symbol guide:**
- \(t\) — time variable
- \([u(t-a) - u(t-b)]\) — the on/off gate for the interval \([a, b]\)
- The expression multiplying the bracket — the local formula for that interval

#### Exam Trigger
When a graph has different formulas on different intervals, multiply each interval's formula by its corresponding step window.

#### Common Misuse
Forgetting the window bracket and letting a line segment extend beyond its intended interval — this produces a signal that is nonzero where it should be zero.

$$x(t)=t\,[u(t)-u(t-2)]-2(t-3)\,[u(t-2)-u(t-3)]$$

### Worked Example: build one formula from a graph

For a graph made of pieces, write one global formula by windowing each piece:

$$x(t)=\sum_k f_k(t)[u(t-a_k)-u(t-b_k)]$$

Recipe:

1. Split the graph into intervals.
2. Write the simple formula \(f_k(t)\) for each interval.
3. Multiply by the window active on that interval.
4. Add all windowed pieces.

Example:

$$x(t)=2[u(t-1)-u(t-3)]-(t-4)[u(t-3)-u(t-5)]$$

This means a height-2 piece from 1 to 3, then a line \(4-t\) from 3 to 5.

Before adding pieces together, it helps to verify the window itself:

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="example_support" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

### Exam Note: differentiating and integrating a plotted signal

When differentiating a jump of size \(\Delta\) at \(t=a\), add \(\Delta\delta(t-a)\). Sloped line segments become constants, and flat segments become 0.

For the running integral
$$y(t)=\int_{-\infty}^{t}x(\tau)d\tau$$
think accumulated area: \(y(t)\) is flat where \(x=0\), increasing where \(x>0\), and decreasing where \(x<0\).

## 4. Reflected steps are left-sided

Replacing \(t\) with \(-t\) inside \(u(\cdot)\) **flips the step around the vertical axis**. The result, \(u(-t)\), is on for all times to the **left** of zero and off for all times to the right.

**Representative examples:**
- \(u(-(-3)) = u(3) = 1\), so \(u(-t)\) evaluated at \(t=-3\) gives 1. ✓ (left of zero)
- \(u(-(2)) = u(-2) = 0\), so \(u(-t)\) evaluated at \(t=2\) gives 0. ✓ (right of zero)

**Related signal:** \(e^{-at}u(-t)\) keeps the exponential active only for \(t \le 0\), because \(u(-t)\) gates out everything for \(t > 0\). This is called a **left-sided signal**.

#### Exam Trigger
\(u(-t)\) in an expression means the signal has **left-sided support** — it lives on \((-\infty, 0]\).

#### Common Misuse
The minus sign is **inside the argument**, so it reverses the time direction. It does **not** make the height of the step negative.

$$u(-t)=\begin{cases}1, & t\le 0\\0, & t>0\end{cases}$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Unit step definition: \(u(t) = 0\) for \(t < 0\) and \(u(t) = 1\) for \(t \ge 0\); it is an instantaneous on-switch, not a ramp.
- Shifted step: \(u(t-a)\) jumps from 0 to 1 at \(t = a\); solve \(t - a = 0\) to find the jump location.
- Rectangular pulse: \(u(t-a) - u(t-b)\) gates the interval from \(a\) to \(b\); after \(t = b\) both steps equal 1 and cancel.
- Piecewise gating: multiply each interval formula by its window \([u(t-a) - u(t-b)]\); example: \(x(t) = t[u(t)-u(t-2)] - 2(t-3)[u(t-2)-u(t-3)]\).
- Reflected step: \(u(-t) = 1\) for \(t \le 0\) and \(0\) for \(t > 0\); the minus sign flips time direction, not amplitude.

*Next, the unit impulse will let us model signals concentrated at a single instant.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
